audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers converged on 'promising' verdict across both cycles. Cycle 1 scores ranged from 6.7 (codex) to 8.0 (gemini) to 7.0 (claude); cycle 2 converged to 6.9/7.0/7.0. The main cross-cycle evolution was gemini revising technical soundness from 8 to 6 after accepting claude's and codex's arguments about the unproved decay premise and unanalyzed H_j growth. Codex's 'major' rating on the l>0 gap was disputed by claude and gemini as overstated relative to the paper's primary contribution. The massive scalar gap was introduced in cycle 2 by claude, accepted by gemini as valid; codex noted it as a scope limitation. No reviewer identified a fatal flaw that would overturn the finite-k core result.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper asks whether extremal black holes — those at the boundary between "charged enough to have one horizon" and "too charged to exist" — can be genuinely stable, or whether they always suffer a slow-burning mathematical instability called the Aretakis effect. The authors show that the worse the instability is for ordinary extremal black holes, the more it can be tamed by making the horizon "more degenerate" (loosely, by forcing more and more derivatives of the metric to vanish there). They then propose a hypothetical geometry with an infinitely degenerate horizon that appears to be free of this instability entirely, offering a candidate for a stable resting state of black hole evolution. The review board found the finite-degeneracy analysis clean and well-supported, but the infinitely-degenerate stability claim rests on an assumption — that the field itself stays bounded — that is only verified numerically for one specific example, not proved analytically.
human_spotchecks: Does the induction argument in Appendix A (Eq. A.20–A.22, via the triangular recursion Eq. A.10) rigorously establish that the blow-up rate for the n-th transverse derivative is exactly t^{n−k} for all n > k, or do off-diagonal contributions from the recursion matrix alter the leading exponent at higher orders?

Is there an existing PDE decay estimate in the mathematical relativity literature for scalar waves on static asymptotically flat spacetimes with degenerate horizons of rank k > 1, or on smooth non-analytic horizons of the type Eq. (3.19), that would supply the missing boundedness/decay premise analytically?

Is the specific infinitely-degenerate metric ansatz (Eq. 4.21, f = exp(−1/r²)) known to be — or approximable as — an on-shell solution of Einstein equations coupled to a physically reasonable matter source (e.g., nonlinear electrodynamics), or is it currently a purely mathematical toy model?

Can l > 0 scalar modes admit subleading near-horizon asymptotics, beyond the leading scaling suppression, that still produce transverse derivative growth on multi-degenerate or infinitely-degenerate horizons?

Do massive scalar fields (or electromagnetic/gravitational perturbations), which lack locally defined Aretakis constants but exhibit Aretakis instability on standard extremal horizons via an alternative mechanism, remain stable on infinitely-degenerate horizons, or does an analogous instability persist?
public_summary: The paper investigates the Aretakis instability — a known pathology of extremal black holes in which transverse derivatives of scalar field perturbations grow without bound along the horizon — for a class of generalized extremal geometries called multi-degenerate horizons. The central analytic result, supported by near-horizon scaling arguments and numerical characteristic evolution, is that the instability weakens systematically as the degree of horizon degeneracy increases: for degeneracy rank k, the first k transverse derivatives of the dominant (s-wave) scalar mode approach finite constants, and only higher-order derivatives exhibit the characteristic polynomial growth. Motivated by this hierarchy, the authors propose an infinitely-degenerate horizon geometry — one for which all radial derivatives of the metric function vanish at the horizon — and argue, with analytic and numerical support, that no transverse derivative blows up at late times for massless scalar perturbations. They connect this to the "black hole graveyard" program, where multi-degenerate horizons are proposed as stable endpoints of black hole evolution that simultaneously evade mass inflation, semiclassical inner-horizon instabilities, and Hawking evaporation. The finite-k screening result is analytically solid and numerically confirmed. The infinitely-degenerate stability claim is physically compelling but conditional: the foundational premise that the field itself does not blow up at late times on these new backgrounds is established only numerically for one specific metric ansatz, not proved with the generality achieved by Aretakis for the standard extremal Reissner-Nordström case. Additional open questions include whether the infinitely-degenerate metric is realizable as an on-shell solution of a physically consistent matter theory, whether stability persists for massive scalars or gravitational perturbations, and whether the infinite sequence of Aretakis constants is spatially regular.
reviewer_disagreements: Severity of the l > 0 decay gap: codex rated the absence of subleading asymptotic control for higher angular modes as 'major' with 'would_change_verdict_if_true: true', arguing the scaling argument does not constitute a decay proof. Claude and gemini regarded it as secondary — the core contribution concerns the l=0 Aretakis softening, and any subleading l>0 growth would be a secondary correction, not a reversal of the central result. This disagreement persisted through both cycles.

Support level for the infinite-k stability claim: gemini rated it 'well_supported' in cycle 1 (with reviewer confidence 9/10 and technical soundness 8/10), while claude and codex rated it 'partially_supported' and flagged the unproved decay premise as a real conditional. Gemini revised downward to 'partially_supported' and technical soundness 6/10 in cycle 2 after accepting the other reviewers' arguments about the boundedness gap and the unanalyzed sequence H_j.

Whether the missing decay theorem is 'major' or 'moderate': codex classed the boundedness assumption as 'major', claude classed it as 'moderate' (and noted it would not change the verdict), while gemini also upgraded it to 'major' in cycle 2 but similarly held that it would not change the verdict. The functional consequence is the same — the result is conditional — but reviewers disagreed on how seriously this undermines the paper's framing.

strongest_objections.objection: The non-blow-up of the scalar field φ itself at late times on the horizon — the foundational premise underpinning all stability and instability conclusions — is assumed rather than proved analytically for the new geometries. For extremal Reissner-Nordström, Aretakis established this rigorously (Theorem 7.2 in [Aretakis:2011hc]). No analogous analytical estimate or cited PDE result is provided for rank-k>1 or infinitely-degenerate horizons; the paper relies on numerical observations for specific initial data and metric choices.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 3.2 (first paragraph after Eq. 3.3); Section 3.4 (premise of the argument); Appendix A (Eq. A.5–A.12)

strongest_objections.objection: For the infinitely-degenerate horizon, the stability argument concludes that all transverse derivatives approach finite constants H_j at late times. However, the growth rate of the infinite sequence {H_j} as j → ∞ is not analyzed. If H_j grows sufficiently fast with j (e.g., as (j!)^α), the spatial field profile φ(v, r) near the horizon could be non-smooth or even divergent in r at late times, even though each individual derivative is bounded in time.

The stability argument for infinitely-degenerate horizons applies only to minimally coupled massless scalar fields, which possess locally defined Aretakis constants. The paper explicitly notes in Section 2.2.2 that massive scalar fields lack locally defined Aretakis constants yet still exhibit Aretakis instability on standard extremal horizons via a different mechanism. Whether the infinitely-degenerate geometry suppresses instability for massive scalars (or electromagnetic and gravitational perturbations) is not analyzed, yet the paper's 'graveyard' framing in the abstract and Section 5 implies a broader classical stability.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 3.4; Section 2.2.2 (massive scalar discussion); Abstract; Section 5

strongest_objections.objection: The near-horizon scaling argument (Section 3.3) establishes that, at leading order in the throat scaling limit, higher angular modes (l > 0) are suppressed. However, subleading corrections to the scaling are not tracked, and it is not analytically ruled out that subleading asymptotics or different scaling weights could still carry derivative growth invisible to the leading-order throat limit. The l > 0 decay claim in the paper's prose is stated more strongly than the scaling argument directly proves.

The infinitely-degenerate metric ansatz (Eq. 3.19; specific example Eq. 4.21, f ~ exp(−1/r²)) is presented without being derived as an on-shell solution of a physically consistent matter theory. The paper cites [DiFilippo:2024spj] and [Gao:2025plm] for finite-k cases where such constructions exist, but no action or matter model supporting the infinite-k geometry is provided.

The numerical simulations lack convergence tests or quantitative error analysis. Grid parameters and domain sizes are reported, but no Richardson extrapolation, grid-refinement comparison, or residual estimates are presented. Figure 4 explicitly acknowledges a 'step-looking behavior' as a numerical artifact, raising the question of whether the late-time saturation of transverse derivatives observed in other figures could also be resolution-dependent.

strongest_supported_claims: For a horizon of degeneracy rank k, the s-wave sector of the scalar wave equation admits k additional Aretakis conservation laws beyond the standard one, arising from the vanishing of the metric function and its first k−1 derivatives at the horizon. This is a local algebraic fact that all reviewers found well-supported (Section 3.2, Eq. 3.3; Appendix A, Eq. A.2–A.5).

The Aretakis instability is a universal, off-shell, local phenomenon governed entirely by the near-horizon throat geometry. The paper cleanly extends the Gralla et al. scaling argument from the AdS_2 throat of standard extremal horizons (k=1) to the Lifshitz throat with dynamical exponent k+1 that emerges for finite multi-degenerate horizons (Sections 2.2.2 and 3.3).

For finite degeneracy rank k, the blow-up pattern is: the first k transverse derivatives of the s-wave scalar approach constants at late times, the (k+1)-th blows up linearly as t, and the n-th (n>k) blows up as t^{n−k}. The recursion derivation in Appendix A is explicit and the numerical results in Figures 1 and 2 match the predicted pattern (Section 3.2, Eq. 3.4; Appendix A, Eq. A.20–A.22).

For k ≥ 2, the centrifugal l(l+1)/r^2 term in the wave equation is suppressed in the near-horizon throat scaling limit relative to the Lifshitz metric factor, so only the l=0 s-wave mode survives at leading order in the throat. This kills the Aretakis-type instability for higher angular modes at the leading scaling level (Section 3.3, Eq. 3.16–3.18; Figures 3 and 6).

Photon sphere stability on the degenerate horizon and the Aretakis instability are distinct phenomena: the photon sphere is always present on a degenerate horizon for any finite k, but its stability (stable for even k on an outer horizon, metastable for odd k, unstable for even k inside an odd-degeneracy outer horizon) is independent of whether the Aretakis mechanism is active (Appendix B, Eq. B.17–B.18).
technical_takeaway: The paper argues that for a static spherically symmetric black hole with a horizon of degeneracy rank k (meaning f and its first k−1 radial derivatives vanish at the horizon), the Aretakis instability for massless minimally coupled scalar probe fields persists but is progressively screened: the first k transverse horizon derivatives of the s-wave mode approach constants at late times, and the (k+1)-th derivative blows up as t^1, with the n-th derivative (n > k) growing as t^{n−k}. This is demonstrated analytically via a near-horizon Aretakis-constant recursion (Appendix A) and via a Lifshitz near-horizon throat scaling argument (Section 3.3), and corroborated numerically for k = 1, 2, 3 and an infinitely-degenerate example. For k ≥ 2, only the l = 0 s-wave mode survives in the leading near-horizon throat limit. For an infinitely-degenerate horizon (f^{(n)}(r_H) = 0 for all n), infinitely many s-wave Aretakis constants prevent any finite-order transverse derivative from blowing up, conditional on the field itself remaining bounded — a premise that is established numerically for one metric (f ~ exp(−1/r²)) but not proved generally. The stability argument is further restricted to massless scalars; the massive scalar case, which lacks locally defined Aretakis constants but still exhibits instability on standard extremal horizons, is not addressed.
